summary:
The primary site where progenitor hematopoietic stem cells develop shifts from the yolk sac initially through the fetal liver; however, postnatal development primarily occurs within the bone marrow which continues this role throughout an individual’s lifetime unless under specific conditions necessitating alternative contributions as seen during acute hemodynamic crises involving extramedullary haematopoeisis among others not directly related clinically speaking but worth noting nonetheless regarding full picture understanding. Summary focusing only on key clinical points:

Progenitor hematopoietic stem cell origins include the yolk sac and fetal liver, yet they mature and function chiefly in the bone marrow after birth, maintaining their critical role into adulthood. Clinical relevance lies particularly emphasizing ongoing production capabilities sustained almost entirely by the bone marrow later stages despite embryonic/early neonatal exceptions noted briefly
